hook: You already know that a triangle has three sides and three angles — but did you know that the *relationship* between those sides and angles can be used to measure the height of a mountain or the distance across a river without ever leaving the ground? Right triangles hold a hidden code that unlocks real-world measurements that seem impossible at first glance. How can knowing just one side and one angle tell you everything about a triangle?
concept: Every topic in this unit is about one powerful idea: in a right triangle, the sides and angles are so tightly connected that knowing just a few facts lets you figure out all the rest — a principle formally captured by trigonometric ratios and the relationships of right-triangle geometry.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: The Pythagorean Theorem and Its Converse
topic_roadmap.description: You will use $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ to find missing side lengths and determine whether a triangle is a right triangle.
Master the built-in side-length ratios of $45°$-$45°$-$90°$ and $30°$-$60°$-$90°$ triangles so you can solve them quickly without a calculator.
You will define sine, cosine, and tangent as ratios of sides — $\sin\theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$, and so on — and evaluate them for angles in a right triangle.
Apply trigonometric ratios and inverse trig functions to find all unknown side lengths and angle measures in any right triangle.
You will model real-world situations — like finding the height of a building or the depth of a valley — by setting up and solving right triangles using angles of elevation and depression.

Extend your trigonometry skills beyond right triangles by using the Law of Sines, $\frac{a}{\sin A} = \frac{b}{\sin B} = \frac{c}{\sin C}$, to solve any triangle given two angles and a side or two sides and a non-included angle.
You will solve triangles in cases the Law of Sines cannot handle — two sides and an included angle, or all three sides — using the Law of Cosines, $c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab\cos C$.
